questions.question: What invariant does an AVL tree enforce?
options: For every node, |height(left) − height(right)| ≤ 1. Strict height balance, restored after every insert/delete via rotations.
questions.explanation: The balance factor bf(N) = h(left) - h(right) is constrained to {-1, 0, +1}. This forces the tree to be 'almost as short as possible' — height ≤ 1.44 log₂(n+2). When an insert or delete makes |bf|=2, we rotate to bring it back to {-1, 0, +1}.

questions.question: How many rotations does an AVL insert require in the worst case?
options: 1 (single rotation, LL/RR) or 2 (double rotation, LR/RL). After rebalancing, the subtree's height returns to what it was before the insert, so no further ancestor is unbalanced.
questions.explanation: Insert pinpoints the imbalance to one node on the way back up. The 1-or-2 rotation fix restores both balance AND height — so ancestors don't need fixing. Delete is harder: it can chain rebalancing up the tree, O(log n) rotations in the worst case.

questions.question: What are the 4 rebalance cases (LL, LR, RR, RL)?
options: First letter = side of unbalance (Left/Right); second letter = side of child's imbalance. LL = single rotate_right; RR = single rotate_left; LR/RL = double rotation (rotate child first, then root).
questions.explanation: LL: parent left-heavy (bf=+2), left-child left-heavy → straight chain, single rotate_right. LR: parent left-heavy, left-child right-heavy → zigzag, must rotate_left on left-child first to straighten, then rotate_right on parent. RR and RL are mirrors. Memorize: 'name describes the chain shape from the unbalanced node'.

questions.question: Why is the AVL height bound exactly 1.44 log₂(n+2) and not 2 log₂(n+2) (as for red-black)?
options: The minimum-node AVL tree of height h has F(h) = F(h-1) + F(h-2) + 1 nodes (Fibonacci recurrence). Solving: h ≤ log_φ(n) ≈ 1.44 log₂(n). AVL's strictness gives a tighter bound than RB.
questions.explanation: AVL's worst-case 'thinnest' tree is the Fibonacci tree: the left and right subtrees of every node differ in height by 1. The recurrence F(h) = F(h-1) + F(h-2) + 1 gives Fib(h+2)−1 nodes. Inverting: h ≤ log_φ(n+2) ≈ 1.44 log₂(n+2). Red-black trees admit thinner-on-one-side trees, so their bound is looser at 2 log₂(n+1).

questions.question: When should you choose AVL over red-black?
options: Read-heavy workloads where lookup latency matters: AVL's tighter height ⇒ ~30% fewer cache misses on lookup. Choose RB for write-heavy: fewer rotations per op.
questions.explanation: AVL's strict balance gives shorter lookups but costs more rotations on writes. RB's looser balance does fewer rotations per write (max 2 vs AVL's max 1, but RB rebalances less often via color flips). Empirical guideline: AVL for read-heavy (search engines, DNS), RB for general-purpose (std::map, Linux kernel). The difference is usually <30% either way; pick by interface fit, not microbenchmark.

questions.question: Which production systems actually use AVL trees?
options: Rarely in production today — RB, B-trees, and skip lists dominate. AVL appears in some embedded systems (libavl), in academic textbooks, and as a stepping-stone for understanding rotations and balance.
questions.explanation: AVL has been overtaken by RB (C++/Java std libs, Linux kernel) and B-trees (databases) in production. Why? RB is slightly cheaper to maintain; B-trees better match cache/page sizes. AVL remains the cleanest demonstration of rotation-based balancing and is the foundation for understanding WAVL, Treaps, and other balanced-BST variants.
